section.title: The Battle with Amalek
section.sectionRationale: Immediately after the rock gives water, Israel faces a fresh threat: Amalek's unprovoked attack at Rephidim. The battle becomes a living picture of the whole Christian life, human effort under Joshua's sword and dependence on God in Moses' raised hands, with Aaron and Hur holding him up when he grows weary.
section.thesis: Amalek attacks a weary, defenseless Israel, and victory comes only as Joshua fights below while Moses' hands, held up by Aaron and Hur, are lifted toward heaven above; the LORD Himself gets the credit and the altar's name, and the passage closes with the hard, honest language of covenant judgment against Amalek for generations to come.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: Amalek's attack and Moses' plan; the battle itself, won only as long as Moses' hands stay lifted with help from Aaron and Hur; and the aftermath, a written memorial, an altar named The LORD Is My Banner, and the sober word of ongoing judgment on Amalek.

verses.text: After this, the {{amalek|Amalekites}} came and attacked the Israelites at Rephidim.
So Moses said to Joshua, “Choose some of our men and go out to fight the Amalekites. Tomorrow I will stand on the hilltop with the staff of God in my hand.”

units.label: Hands lifted, hands held up
units.note: as long as Moses held up his hands, Israel prevailed

verses.text: Joshua did as Moses had instructed him and fought against the Amalekites, while Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill.
As long as Moses held up his hands, Israel prevailed; but when he lowered them, Amalek prevailed.

When Moses’ hands grew heavy, they took a stone and put it under him, and he sat on it. Then Aaron and Hur held his hands up, one on each side, so that his hands remained steady until the sun went down.
So Joshua overwhelmed Amalek and his army with the sword.
Then the LORD said to Moses, “Write this on a scroll as a reminder and recite it to Joshua, because I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven.”
And Moses built an altar and named it {{banner|The LORD Is My Banner}}.
“Indeed,” he said, “a hand was lifted up toward the {{throne|throne}} of the LORD. The LORD will war against Amalek from generation to generation.”

amalek.gloss: Amalek, a grandson of Esau, Genesis 36:12, and so a distant kinsman turned enemy. His unprovoked attack on the weary and defenseless, Deuteronomy 25:17-18, marks Amalek as a byword for cruel opposition to God's redeemed people.

banner.gloss: My banner, my rallying standard. A nes was a raised signal by which scattered troops could see where to gather; naming the altar Yahweh Nissi, the LORD is my banner, Moses gives all credit for the victory to God rather than to Israel's own strength.

throne.gloss: Throne, here an unusual shortened form of the ordinary word kisse, forming a wordplay with Yah, the shortened divine name. The line is among the most compressed in the Hebrew Bible, but its sense is plain: this war belongs finally to the LORD.

basic.context: Fresh from water at the rock, Israel is attacked without warning by Amalek, distant kin through Esau, who strikes the weary and straggling at the rear, Deuteronomy 25:17-18. Moses sends Joshua to fight while he climbs the hill with the staff of God, and the battle turns entirely on whether his hands stay lifted.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real, remembered history, a specific battle at Rephidim, ordered to be written down so it would not be forgotten. It teaches, by its very structure, that human effort, Joshua's sword, and dependence on God, Moses' lifted hands upheld by others, belong together, not as competitors but as one whole obedience.
basic.originalLanguages: Amalek's name marks him as a kinsman turned enemy, grandson of Esau. Moses names the altar Yahweh Nissi, the LORD is my banner, my rallying point, refusing any credit for the victory. The closing line is famously compressed in Hebrew, a hand raised to the kes, throne, of Yah, language ancient readers already found difficult, but its thrust is plain: this war belongs to the LORD, not to Israel's strength.
basic.typology: Moses' raised hands, upheld to the very end of the battle by Aaron and Hur, anticipate the ongoing, unwearied intercession of Christ, our great high priest, who is never too weary to hold up His people, and who, unlike Moses, needs no one to hold up His hands.
basic.christ: Moses stands on the hill interceding while Joshua, whose name is the Hebrew form of Jesus, fights and wins the battle below; Scripture later shows the greater Joshua, Jesus, winning the decisive battle against sin and death while His people are carried by His unfailing intercession, Hebrews 7:25.
basic.god: God fights for His people through ordinary means, a sword in Joshua's hand and a staff in Moses', while making unmistakably clear that the victory and the glory are His alone; He also speaks in the hard language of covenant judgment against Amalek, showing that His patience with unprovoked cruelty toward His people is not unlimited.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 25:17-19 recalls Amalek's cowardly attack and commands Israel to remember and, in time, blot out his memory. 1 Samuel 15 shows Saul's failure to carry out that command. Hebrews 7:25 pictures Christ's unceasing intercession, of which Moses' upheld hands are a small, weary shadow. 1 Timothy 2:8 echoes lifted hands as the posture of prayer.
basic.oneStory: Israel's rescue is never finished with the exodus alone; it must be defended, generation after generation, against enemies who will not let God's redeemed people rest. The pattern set here, human effort joined to God given, God sustained intercession, points to Christ, who fights and prays for His people at once, and who will one day put every enemy, however old and however patient His judgment, finally under His feet.

context.literary: The scene alternates deliberately between the plain below, where Joshua fights, and the hilltop above, where Moses, Aaron, and Hur stand; the narrative's rhythm, hands up, Israel prevails, hands down, Amalek prevails, is the whole point, made almost liturgical by repetition.
context.historical: Amalek was a seminomadic people descended from Esau, Genesis 36:12, whose raid targeted Israel's weak and weary stragglers rather than meeting them in open battle, Deuteronomy 25:17-18. This becomes the first of a long history of Amalekite hostility toward Israel, continuing through Saul, 1 Samuel 15, and echoing, some argue, into Haman the Agagite in Esther.
context.canonical: The command to write this down as a memorial and recite it to Joshua marks this as one of Scripture's earliest instances of Israel being told to preserve a specific memory in writing, an instinct that shapes the whole biblical practice of remembering God's acts, for both comfort and warning.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Israel's victory over Amalek came neither from Joshua's swordsmanship alone nor from Moses' hands alone, but from the LORD, credited by the altar's name, while marking Amalek's unprovoked cruelty for lasting judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific battle and a specific, unrepeated command concerning Amalek; it does not prescribe holy war against any nation today. Its abiding principle, carried into the New Testament, is the need for both faithful action and persistent, supported prayer, and the sober truth that God does not forget cruelty toward His people.

debates.question: How should Christians read the LORD's declared, generational war against Amalek and its later outworking under Saul?

views.label: As specific, time-bound covenant judgment
views.case: This is a particular sentence on a particular nation for a particular act of unprovoked cruelty toward Israel at a vulnerable moment, executed within Israel's own redemptive history and not a template for later warfare.

views.label: As an illustration of God's settled opposition to all that opposes His redemptive purposes
views.case: Others read Amalek typologically, standing for every persistent, generational enmity against God's people, ultimately answered not by Israel's sword but by Christ's final victory over every enemy, 1 Corinthians 15:25.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text does not license personal vengeance or holy war today; it points to God's own justice, worked out in His own time, and finally at the cross and the last judgment.

study.originalLanguages: Amalek is named as a grandson of Esau, so this attack is, in a real sense, family turned enemy, striking those God had promised to bless. Moses' altar, Yahweh Nissi, the LORD is my banner, borrows military language; a nes was a rallying standard raised so scattered troops could see where to gather, and Moses uses it to say plainly that the LORD Himself was Israel's rallying point in the battle. The final line is among the most compressed in the Hebrew Bible, a hand toward the kes, throne, of Yah, a phrase ancient translators already wrestled with, yet its meaning is not in doubt: this war belongs finally to God.

typology.type: Moses' hands held up by Aaron and Hur
typology.fulfillment: A picture of ongoing intercession that anticipates Christ's own unceasing intercession for His people, Hebrews 7:25, an intercession that, unlike Moses', never grows weary and needs no one to hold it up.

typology.type: Joshua fighting below while Moses intercedes above
typology.fulfillment: Joshua, whose Hebrew name Jesus later bears, wins by the sword under Moses' intercession, foreshadowing the greater Joshua who both fights for His people and intercedes for them at once.

study.christ: Joshua bears the very name later given to Jesus, and here he wins a battle he could not have won without Moses' hands lifted above him, a picture completed in Christ, who fights for His people against sin and death and intercedes for them without ever growing weary or needing another to hold up His hands, Hebrews 7:25, Romans 8:34.
study.god: God gives victory through ordinary means, a sword and a staff, while making sure Israel credits Him alone, the altar's very name refusing any boast in human strength. At the same time God speaks in the hard, honest language of covenant judgment on Amalek, showing that cruelty toward His people, however long deferred, does not go unanswered.

crossReferences.note: Recalls Amalek's cowardly attack on the weary and straggling, and commands Israel to blot out his memory once settled in the land.
God commands Saul to carry out judgment on Amalek for this very attack, generations later; Saul's partial obedience there costs him the kingdom.
Christ always lives to intercede for His people, an unwearied intercession of which Moses' upheld hands are only a faint, weary shadow.
Echoes the posture of lifted hands as the shape of persistent prayer for believers.
Christ must reign until He has put all enemies under His feet, the final answer to every Amalek that opposes God's people.

study.oneStory: God's redeemed people are never simply left to enjoy their rescue; they must be defended, and defended in a way that joins ordinary obedience, Joshua's sword, to utterly dependent prayer, Moses' lifted hands. That pattern runs on to Christ, the greater Joshua, who fights for His people and intercedes for them without weariness, and who will one day finish what began at Rephidim by putting every enemy under His feet.

quiz.q: Why did Amalek's attack on Israel deserve special condemnation, according to Deuteronomy 25:17-18?

options.text: Because Amalek struck the weary and straggling from behind, attacking those too exhausted to defend themselves.
options.note: Deuteronomy 25:17-18 gives this exact reason for Amalek's lasting condemnation.

options.text: Because Amalek refused to let Israel pass through their territory.
options.note: That is the complaint against Edom and others, not the specific charge against Amalek here.

options.text: Because Amalek worshiped different gods than Israel.
options.note: The stated reason is Amalek's cruelty toward the weary and defenseless, not a dispute over worship.

options.text: No reason for the attack is ever given in Scripture.
options.note: Deuteronomy 25:17-18 explicitly explains why Amalek's attack was so condemned.

quiz.q: What determined whether Israel or Amalek prevailed in the battle?

options.text: Whether Moses kept his hands lifted up; Israel prevailed when they were up and Amalek prevailed when they were down.
options.note: Verse 11 states this directly.

options.text: The number of soldiers Joshua chose to fight.
options.note: The text ties the outcome to Moses' hands, not the size of Joshua's force.

options.text: The time of day the battle was fought.
options.note: Time of day is not what the text credits; it is Moses' lifted hands.

options.text: The battle's outcome had nothing to do with Moses at all.
options.note: The text ties the outcome directly and repeatedly to Moses' raised hands.

quiz.q: Who helped Moses when his hands grew heavy, and how?

options.text: Aaron and Hur, who held his hands up on each side until sunset while he sat on a stone.
options.note: Verse 12 describes this exactly.

options.text: Joshua, who left the battle to come and help him.
options.note: Joshua remained in the battle below; Aaron and Hur held up Moses' hands.

options.text: The elders of Israel, who prayed at the base of the hill.
options.note: The text names Aaron and Hur specifically as the ones who held up Moses' hands.

options.text: No one helped Moses; he held his own hands up unaided the entire day.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly says Aaron and Hur held his hands up.

quiz.q: What did Moses name the altar he built after the victory, and why?

options.text: The LORD Is My Banner, giving credit for the victory to God rather than to Israel's own strength.
options.note: Verse 15 records this name directly.

options.text: Ebenezer, marking a stone of help.
options.note: Ebenezer belongs to a later victory in 1 Samuel 7, not to this altar.

options.text: Bethel, the house of God.
options.note: Bethel is the name Jacob gave a different site in Genesis, not this altar.

options.text: The altar was left unnamed in the text.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly gives the altar's name.

quiz.q: What did the LORD declare regarding Amalek in verses 14 and 16?

options.text: That He would utterly blot out the memory of Amalek and would war against Amalek from generation to generation.
options.note: Verses 14 and 16 state this declaration directly.

options.text: That Amalek would be shown mercy and welcomed into Israel.
options.note: The declaration is one of lasting judgment, the opposite of welcome or mercy.

options.text: That Amalek's descendants would become priests in Israel.
options.note: No such role is given to Amalek; the declared word is one of judgment.

options.text: That the LORD would never mention Amalek again.
options.note: The opposite is true; Moses is told to write this down precisely so it will be remembered.

questions: Amalek attacked Israel at their weariest and most vulnerable moment. Where do you find yourself most vulnerable to spiritual attack right now, and how are you guarding that place?
Israel's victory needed both Joshua's sword and Moses' lifted hands; neither alone was enough. Where might you be relying only on effort, or only on prayer, when God calls you to both?
Moses grew weary and needed Aaron and Hur to hold up his hands. Who holds up your hands when you grow weary in prayer or service, and whose hands are you holding up?
Moses named the altar for God's help, not Israel's strength. What victory in your own life do you need to rename, giving credit to God rather than to yourself?

The text is honest that God's judgment on Amalek would take generations to complete. How do you hold onto trust in God's justice when His timing for setting things right is far slower than you would like?

translationNotes.note: The staff of God is the same staff Moses used to strike the Nile and the rock, Exodus 7:20, 17:6; carrying it up the hill ties this battle to the same divine power at work in Israel's earlier deliverances.

The LORD Is My Banner renders the Hebrew Yahweh Nissi; a nes was a raised standard or rallying signal, so the name declares that God Himself, not Israel's army, was the true rallying point of the victory.

The closing line is one of the most compressed and debated in the Hebrew Bible, literally a hand upon the kes, a shortened form of throne, of Yah, a shortened form of the divine name; translators have long wrestled with its exact grammar, but its sense, that this war belongs to the LORD across generations, is clear.
